coredata.dc:title: Sn(IV) porphyrin-biotin decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots nanohybrids for photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Magaela, N. Bridged
Matshitse, Refilwe
Babu, Balaji
Managa, Muthumuni
Prinsloo, Earl
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a minimally invasive therapeutic procedure for cancer treatment. This study focuses on the synthesis, photophysicochemical properties, and PDT activity of Sn (IV) porphyrin (2), when linked to biotin decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (B-NGQDs). The porphyrin complex 2 was conjugated through an ester bond to B-NGQDs to form 2-B-NGQDs. Singlet oxygen quantum yield increased for 2 when linked to B-NGQDs to form 2-B-NQGDs. The dark toxicity and photodynamic therapy studies were conducted for 2, NGQDs and their conjugates using MCF-7 breast cancer cells. The cell viability for dark toxicity of all the compounds was above 90%, and 2-B-NGQDs showed high PDT activity at a concentration of 40 µg/mL with cell viability of 22%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tin porphyrin
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Morpholine
Biotin
Photodynamic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of 5,10,15,20 tetrakis-4-morpholinyl porphyrin, complex 1, Scheme 1
Synthesis of Sn(IV) 5,10,15,20 tetrakis-4-morpholinyl porphyrin, complex 2, Scheme 1
Conjugation of B-NGQDs to Sn (IV) porphyrin, Scheme 3
Synthesis and characterization of the porphyrins
TEM images, DLS and zeta potential

Florescence quantum yield (
ϕ
F), life-times (τF) and triplet lifetimes (τT)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology ( UID 62620 ) and Rhodes University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV/Vis electronic absorption spectra of (A) complexes 1 and 2 and (B) complex 2, NGQDs, B-NGQDs and 2-B-NGQDs using DMSO as a solvent.
TEM images of NGQDs, B-NGQDs, and 2-B-NGQDs.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) distribution curve for NGQDs and its conjugates.
EDX spectra analysis of NGQDs, 2, and their conjugates.
XRD analysis for the NGQDs and its conjugates.
FT-IR spectra of NGQDs, B-NGQDs, 2-B-NGQDs and biotin.
XPS Wide scans of (A) NGQDs, B-NGQDs. XPS high resolution spectra for NGQDs and B-NGQDs: (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) O1s.
Raman spectra of NGQDs and its conjugates.
Degradation of 9,10-dimethylanthracene (DMA) in the presence of (A) 2-B-NGQDs and (B) complex 2 using DMSO as a solvent.
Histograms showing (A) Dark toxicity and (B) PDT activity of 2, NGQDs and their conjugates. Irradiation for at 625 nm for 30 min for (B).
MCF-7 cellular uptake over 24 h at different concentrations.
Synthetic pathway of complex 1, and complex 2.
DLS size, Zeta potential (in H2O) and photophysiochemical (in DMSO) parameters of complex 2, NGQDs and conjugates.
IC50 values of 2, NGQDs and their conjugates against MCF-7 cells.

head.ce:title: Sn(IV) porphyrin-biotin decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots nanohybrids for photodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Makhanda, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Makhanda, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy activity of Sn (IV) porphyrin, significantly improved when linked to biotin decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum due to specificity and targeting introduced by the latter.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a minimally invasive therapeutic procedure for cancer treatment. This study focuses on the synthesis, photophysicochemical properties, and PDT activity of Sn (IV) porphyrin (2), when linked to biotin decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (B-NGQDs). The porphyrin complex 2 was conjugated through an ester bond to B-NGQDs to form 2-B-NGQDs. Singlet oxygen quantum yield increased for 2 when linked to B-NGQDs to form 2-B-NQGDs. The dark toxicity and photodynamic therapy studies were conducted for 2, NGQDs and their conjugates using MCF-7 breast cancer cells. The cell viability for dark toxicity of all the compounds was above 90%, and 2-B-NGQDs showed high PDT activity at a concentration of 40 µg/mL with cell viability of 22%.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is currently used as an alternative treatment for cancer, and there is evidence that suggests that PDT has the potential to be widely applicable to a wide range of human cancer [1]. PDT is a minimally invasive treatment modality for diseased cancer cells, which uses laser light of appropriate wavelength, a viable photosensitizer to generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS, including singlet oxygen) to eradicate diseased cells [2].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials that are photostable, biocompatible, soluble in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents, minimally toxic, photoluminescent, and are excellent electron donors [3]. The presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on GQDs surface and edges enable covalent attachment, electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding with other suitable moieties [4]. GQDs have been extensively used for targeting and delivery of therapeutic agents to the tumor sites [5]. Doping GQDs with nitrogen to form NGQDs not only increases the charge mobility of the graphitic lattice, but also lowers the energy band gap [6]. In this work NGQDs are linked to biotin (to give B-NGQDs), through an amide bond and the conjugate is linked a Sn(IV) porphyrin (complex 2) axially through an ester bond (to give 2-B-NGQDs). The resulting conjugate is employed for PDT.

Biotin is a micronutrient that is required for cellular functions and specifically for cell growth, it is present in larger amounts in cancer cells than in normal cells [7]. Therefore, attaching biotin to a drug that is for cancer treatment helps the drug to locate in the cancer cells. Biotin has been linked to pristine (not N doped) GQDs followed by adsorption of a phthalocyanine (related to porphyrins) [8]. The current work reports for the first time on linking of a porphyrin to B-NGQDs, via an ester bond.

Porphyrins are aromatic macrocyclic structures that are well known as photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy (PDT) [9]. GQDs are also known PDT agents [10]. A combination of two PDT agents (porphyrins and GQDs) has been previously shown to improve PDT through synergistic effect [11]. In this work, a porphyrin containing morpholine complex 2 is employed, morpholine is a known anticancer agent [12]. Porphyrins asymmetrically substituted with morpholine ligands are known and their use for PDT has been reported [13]. However, Cu was employed as a central metal in the literature report. In this work we use a heavy Sn(IV) central metal. The heavy Sn(IV) encourages intersystem crossing to the triplet state, resulting in improved PDT activity [14]. The Sn(IV) porphyrin will then be conjugated to B-NGQDs through an ester bond.

N,N-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 4-dimethylamino pyridine (DMAP), biotin, ethanol, methanol (MeOH), pyrrole, pyridine, propanoic acid, 4-(4-morpholinyl) benzaldehyde, tin (IV) chloride, ammonia, deuterated chloroform (CDCl3), dimethyl formamide (DMF), chloroform, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Zn 5,10,15,20-tetraphenyl-21H,23H-porphine (ZnTPP), 9,10 dimethylanthracene (DMA), LUDOX HS-40 colloidal silica, and alpha-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid, were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The synthesis of NQGDs [15] has been reported.

Human breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7 were purchased from Cellonex®. The Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffer saline, Dulbecco’s modified eagle’s medium (DMEM, containing 4.5 g/ l-glucose with l-glutamine) with and without phenyl-red were purchased from Lonza®. The heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10 % (v/v)) and the 100 μg/ mL-penicillin:100 unit/ mL-streptomycin-amphotericin-B-mixture (PSA) as well as the neutral red cell proliferation reagent (WST) were all purchased from Biowest®.

Ultraviolet visible (UV/Vis) optical absorption spectroscopy was recorded using Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer, mass spectral analysis was performed using Brucker AutoFLEX III smartbeam TOF/TOF Mass spectrometer using a positive ion mode using alpha-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid as a MALDI matrix. Fluorescence emission spectra was recorded using Varian Eclipse spectrofluorometer, transmission electron microscope (TEM) pictures were recorded using JOEL JEM 1210cat 100 kV acceleration voltage, infra-red spectra were recorded using a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR spectrometer with a platinum-ATR, proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded using Bruker EMX600 MHz NMR spectrometer, dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to measure particle size distribution and zeta potential of nanoparticles and nanoconjugates using a Malvern Zetasizer Nanoscience. Nano-ZS90, merck Eppendorf centrifuge 5810 was used for precipitates extraction. Merck Eppendorf centrifuge 5810 used for extraction. For phase identification of the materials, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using Bruker D8, with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å, nickel filter), details have been provided before [16]. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX, INCA PENTA FET coupled with VAGA TESCAM operated at 20 kV) was utilized to qualitatively determine the elemental compositions.

Photo-irradiations for singlet oxygen studies (and triplet lifetimes determinations) were done using a Spectra-Physics Quanta Ray Indi-40-10 (118 mJ @ 355 nm, 7 ns, 10 Hz) Nd:YAG laser to pump a Spectra-Physics primoScan OPO (405–2855 nm, 39 mJ @ 430 nm). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time-correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 300, PicoquantR GmbH) with a diode laser (LDHPC420, 420 nm, 20 MHz repetition rate, 44 ps pulse width) in a manner described previously [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of 5,10,15,20 tetrakis-4-morpholinyl porphyrin, complex 1, Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para:
Propanoic acid (150 mL) was refluxed in the presence of 4-(-4 morpholinyl) benzaldehyde (2 g, 0.01 mol) at 120 °C. This was followed by the addition of pyrrole (1 mL) and the mixture was allowed to reflux for 3 h, then cooled to room temperature to precipitate the porphyrin. The product was then flittered and washed with methanol and air dried to give complex 1 as a purple solid. Yield = 78%. FTIR (cm−1): 2995 (Ar, CH), 2800(-CH), 1600 (Ar, CN), 1200 (COC). 1H NMR (CDCI3):
δ
ppm 8.43 (d, 4H, Ar, pyrrole), 8.31 (s, 4H, Ar, pyrrole), 7.44(d, 8H, Ar, phenyl) 7.34 (d, 8H, Ar, phenyl), 3.45 (m, 16H, CH2), 1.20 (m, 16H, CH2). MALDI-TOF MS, calc 954.12, found 956.46 [M + 2H]+. UV/Vis (DMSO), λmax nm (log ε): 428 (4.21), 522 (3.85), 562 (3.73), 598 (3.57), 652 (3.32).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Sn(IV) 5,10,15,20 tetrakis-4-morpholinyl porphyrin, complex 2, Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para:
The 5,10,15,20 tetra-4-morpholinyl porphyrin (1) (0.1 g, 0.0001 mol) and tin(IV) chloride (0.2 g, 0.001 mol) were added to pyridine (20 mL) and allowed to reflux for 3 h. The mixture was cooled down to room temperature, and ammonia (50 mL) was added to the reaction followed by stirring for 1 h at room temperature. The mixture was then filtered and washed with water and air dried to give Sn(OH)2tetra-4-morpholinyl porphyrin (complex 2) as a green solid, alumina neutral column with MeOH/CHCI3 (1:9) as eluent was used to obtain a pure complex 2. Yield = 62%, FTIR (cm−1) 2900 (Ar,CH), 2800 (CH), 1661 (Ar,CN), 1221 (CN), 1150 (COC). 1H NMR (, CDCI3):
δ
ppm 9.20 (s, Ar,4H, pyrrole), 9.14 (s, Ar, 4H, pyrrole), 8.25 (s, 8H, Ar, phenyl), 7.35 (d, 8H, Ar, phenyl), 4.0 (m, 16H, CH2), 1.8 (m,16H, CH2). MALDI-TOF MS, calc 1106.35, found 1105.35 [M + H]+. UV/Vis (DMSO), λmax nm (log ε):434 (4.29), 568 (3.64), 625 (3.61).

Biotin (40 g, 0.16 mmol) was dissolved in DMF (5 mL), and to this solution DCC (23 mg, 0.18mmol) and DMAP (20 mg, 0.16mmol) were added and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. NGQDs (0.2 g) were added to water (2 mL) followed by sonication for 30 min. The biotin solution was then added to the NGQDs solution. The mixture was stirred for 24 h at room temperature, the biotin-NGQDs (B-NGQDs) mixture was suspended in ethanol and then collected by centrifugation to remove the non-conjugated biotin, NGQDs, DCC and DMAP [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of B-NGQDs to Sn (IV) porphyrin, Scheme 3
ce:section.ce:para: Sn(IV) porphyrin (2) (40 mg, 0.03 mmol) and B-NGQDs (20 mg) were dissolved in dry DMF and sonicated for 4 h. The mixture was then refluxed with continuous stirring in the dark for 72 h, the conjugates were precipitated using ethanol and collected by centrifugation and thereafter dried in vacuum [18].

For cell studies, the human breast cancer MCF7-cells were cultured in 75 cm2 vented flasks (or 96-well plates, for cytotoxicity studies) in a 37 ͦC incubator from HealForce® with a ∼ 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere in culture media (DMEM containing 4.5 g/ l-glucose with l-glutamine and phenol red, supplemented with 10% FBS and 5% PSA). To determine drug content in cell samples, absorbances were obtained using the SpectraMax M3, Molecular Devices multi-well-plate reader from Separations using the SoftMax Pro6.4 programme [19]. Irradiation was with a Thorlabs M625L3 LED (625 nm) for 30 min to evaluate the photodynamic therapy activity of the complex and conjugates.

The porphyrin complexes, NGQDs, B-NGQDs and 2-B-NGQDs were characterized using UV/Vis spectroscopy, MALTI-TOF mass spectra, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), 1H NMR, transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM), and dynamic light scattering (DLS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the porphyrins
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular peak ion was observed at m/z = 956.12 for complex 1 that is [M + 2H]+of what was expected, and at m/z = 1105.35 for complex 2 that is [M + H]+ of what was expected, Fig. S1 (supporting Information). 1H NMR spectrum for complex 1 showed 56H that is –2H of what was expected, these 2H are from the inner NH protons, and it is reported in literature that usually the inner NH protons of porphyrins are strongly shielded due to the ring currents, reflecting the aromaticity of porphyrins [20]. Complex 2 the number of protons obtained were 56H being shot of 2H from the central metal and this is due to the fact that these protons are highly shielded [21], Fig. S2.
NGQDs were linked to biotin to form B-NGQDs via an amide bond, Scheme 2. B-NGQDs were then linked to complex 2 through an ester bond as shown in Scheme 3.

After covalent linking of biotin to the NGQDs the absorption of biotin decorated NGQDs (B-NGQDs) was observed at 331 nm which blue shifted by 6 nm compared to NGQDs alone which absorbed at 337 nm, Fig. 1B, Table 1. The peak due to B-NGQDs in the conjugate (2-B-NGQDs) was observed at 314 nm which is blue shifted by 17 nm compared to B-NGQDs at 331 nm. The blue shift is evidence of strong interaction between the porphyrin and GQDs [25]. A red shift on the Soret band of 2-B-NGQDs compared to complex 2 alone was also observed and this could be due to close packing attributed to the orientation of metalloporphyrins on the surface of the nanoparticles [26–28]. The B-NGQDs showed weaker fluorescence compared NGQDs, due to the presence of heavier atoms such as S in biotin Fig. S3 (Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM images, DLS and zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used for morphological analysis of the NGQDs, B-NGQDs, 2-B-NGQDs. TEM images were used to estimate the shape, size distribution and particle sizes of NGQDs, B-NGQDs, and 2-B-NGQDs, Fig. 2 . It was observed that the NGQDs were circular in shape, and there was size increase upon the covalent linking of biotin and NGQDs and upon conjugation with the porphyrin as well.

The average particle size distribution of NGQDs, B-NGQDs and 2-B-NGQDs was obtained by dynamic light scattering (DLS). The average diameter of NGQDs obtained was 4.2 nm, for B-NGQDs was 8.7 nm and for 2-B-NGQDs was 28.0 nm, Fig. 3 , Table 1. Upon the conjugation there was a huge increase in size, and this is due to aggregation. Aggregation is probably due to interactions between the porphyrins on adjacent B-NGQDs.

Zeta potential is a measure of charge carried by particles suspended in a liquid, it indicates the stability of colloidal dispersion. The degree of electrostatic repulsion between adjacent particles is indicated by the magnitude of zeta potential. The high zeta potential value indicates the stability, and the smaller value indicates that attractive forces exceeds repulsion which results in aggregation [29]. The zeta potentials of NGQDs obtained was (−7 mV), B-NGQDs (−21 mV), and 2-B-NGQDs (–23 mV), Table 1, an increase was observed following the conjugation of biotin to NGQDs and of 2 to B-NGQDs, implying improved stability.

EDX spectra analysis was used to find the elemental compositions for complexes 1, 2 and NGQDs, B-NGQDs as well as the 2-B-NGQDs, Fig. 4 . For the NGQDs, the Na peak was observed due to that sodium hydroxide that was used in the synthesis to neutralize the pH of the NGQDs. Complex 2 and the conjugate showed the expected elemental peaks, and upon the metalation of complex 1, Sn peak was observed in complex 2 which shows the presence of Sn. The decoration of NGQDs with biotin resulted in the appearance of the S peak which confirms the presence of biotin in NGQDS. In the conjugate (2-B-NGQDs) all the peaks observed in complex 2 and in B-NGQDs were observed.

NGQDs exhibited a broad diffraction at 2
θ
= 30 which is ascribed to Bragg’s reflection of the carbon in the graphene layers [30], Fig. 5 . The B-NGQDs exhibited a diffraction at 2
θ
= 17°, thus the presence of biotin in NGQDs caused a shift to lower values. Complex 2 exhibited a broad peak at 2
θ
= 28° due to the amorphous nature of the porphyrin [31]. The conjugates (2-B-NGQDs) showed a peak at 2
θ
= 19° which is shifted to lower values when compared to B-NGQDs peak by 2 units. Shifts in the XRD patterns of graphene materials have been associated with the formation of new material [32].

To confirm the functional groups present, FT-IR spectra was obtained, Fig. 6 . The covalent linking of biotin and NGQDs (to form B-NGQDs) was confirmed by the presence of the amide vibration at 1645 cm−1 and an OH from the NGQDs at 3319 cm−1. Upon conjugation of B-NGQDs to complex 2, there is a shift of NH strech (for B-NGQDs) to 1656 cm−1 and there is a presence of COC peak at 1472 which confirms succesful conjugation of complex 2 and B-NGQDs through the ester bond. Shifts in FT-IR spectra imply the presence of molecular interactions [33].
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out to probe the structural compositions of the NGQDs and functionalised biotin, Fig. 7 .

Wide scan XPS spectrum of NGQDs in Fig. 7 shows C, N, and O (1s) signals at 285.2, 396.9 eV and 531.9 and, respectively (Fig. 7A). Na auger peak at 494.4 eV as the result of NaOH employed in the NGQDs synthesis. The O (1s) peak is related to ester and hydroxyl groups bonded to aromatics which is the case on the NGQDs and B-NGQDs [34]. The C 1s peak of NGQDs can be deconvoluted into peaks centred at 284.5 (CC, CN, CO), 285.0 (C-OH), 286.4 (CN) and 288.2 eV (CO) [35]. The CN peak at 286.4 eV indicates that N is covalently bonded to C which suggest successful doping [36]. The C 1s peak of B-NGQDs can be deconvoluted into peaks centred at 284.5 eV (CC, CN, CO, CS), 285.9 eV (C-OH), 287.9 eV (COC) [35]. The N 1s peaks of NGQDs can be deconvoluted into peaks centred at 398.0, and 399.1, corresponding to pyridinic (398.0 eV) [36], amino (399.1) [30]. The XPS N 1s peak for B-NGQDs can be deconvoluted into peaks centred at 398.0. 399.4 and 401.2 eV, corresponding to pyridinic [30], OC-N [37], and NH [38,39], respectively, Fig. 7C.

The pyridinic composition is dominant relative to amino functional group for NGQDs. The disappearance of primary amine functional groups NH2 (for NGQDs) to NH (for B-NGQDs) confirm the successful formation of the amide bond and the formation of B-NGQDs. The O 1s peak of NGQDs can be deconvoluted into peaks centred at 590.0 eV (metal oxide from Na auger), 530.4 eV (CO), and 533.1 eV (COC), and the O 1s peaks of B-NGQDs can deconvoluted into peaks centred at 528.9 eV (metal oxide from Na auger), 530.2 eV (CO), and 533.1 (COC).
Raman spectroscopy was used to determine the degree of structural defects introduced on the surface of the NGQDs upon conjugation to biotin and porphyrin complex, Fig. 8 .

The G-band is a representation of in-plane vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, and D band is due to out of plane vibration sp3 attributed to the presence of structural defects [40]. The disorder (D, sp3) defects and graphitic (G, sp2) bands were observed respectively at (1374; 1570 cm−1) for NGQDs, (1286; 1574 cm−1) for B-NGQDs, and (1291; 1602 cm−1) for 2-B-NGQDs. Upon the conjugation to B-NGQDs and complex 2, the D and G bands shifted. Raman shifts have been attributed to factors such as nature, diameter and strain of nanoparticles [41–44]. The ID/IG of NGQDs obtained was 0.86, for B-NGQDs it increased to 0.93 and it increased drastically upon the conjugation of biotin and porphyrin complex (2-B-NGQDs) to 2.57. This is due to introduction of defects.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Florescence quantum yield (
ϕ
F), life-times (τF) and triplet lifetimes (τT)
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence quantum yield (
ϕ
F) were obtained using ZnTPP as a standard (
ϕ
F std = 0.030) as a standard [45] in DMSO for porphyrins complexes, following the comparative methods reported in literature [46]. Quinine sulfate in 0.05 M in H2SO4 solution (ϕ F = 0.52) [47], was used as a standard for NGQDs. The ϕ F value for NGQDs (ϕ F = 0.70) is quite high. The ϕ F values of complex 2 (ϕ F =
< 0.01 )
, B-NGQDs (ϕ F = 0.01) and 2-B-NGQDs (conjugates ϕ F =
< 0.01
) were observed to be relatively low. The low yields can be explained in terms of the heavy atom effect of Sn(IV) in complex 2 [48] and sulfur [49] in B-NGQDs which favors the intersystem crossing other than fluorescence. The fluorescence life-times (
τ
F) of complexes and conjugates were studied using TCSPC analysis by excitation at emission maxima. NGQDs gave a high fluorescent lifetime (
τ
F = 5.02 ns). The
τ
F values are low for B-NGQDs, complex 2 and the conjugate, for the reasons given above for ϕ F values. The triplet decay curves for complex 2 and 2-B-NGQDs are shown in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information) and were used for the determination of triplet lifetimes. Complex 2 gave
τ
T = 20 μs and 2-B-NGQDs (
τ
T = 31 μs) which was higher than of complex 2. Lengthening of triplet lifetimes 2-B-NGQDs could be due to protection of the pophyrins by the B-NGQDs.

Singlet oxygen is generated through an energy transfer between the excited triplet state of photosensitizer and ground state in molecular oxygen [50]. In this study, the
ϕ Δ
value was determined by monitoring chemical degradation of DMA as a singlet oxygen quencher and ZnTPP (
ϕ Δ
std = 0.53) as a standard in DMSO [45]. The degradation of DMA was observed, (Fig. 9 ) and the values of
ϕ Δ
were calculated using the equation reported in literature [51]. The singlet oxygen quantum yields for complex 2 of
ϕ Δ
= 0.59 was obtained for 2-B-NGQDs,
ϕ Δ
= 0.79 was obtained which was higher than of complex 2 and it was expected because there is presence of heavy atoms like sulfur from the B-NGQDs and the presence of the heavy metal.

The stock concentrations of each of the complexes (2), nanoparticles (NGQDs, B-NGQDs) and their conjugates (2-B-NGQDs) were prepared by dissolving each in 1% (v/v) DMSO and making the volume up with supplemented media. Complex 2 and conjugate 2-B-NGQDs were partially soluble in water so to make them completely soluble 1% DMSO was added. The 1% (v/v) DMSO (vehicle control) had a negligible effect on the cells as showed in Fig. 10 (A and B) at concentration of 0 µg/mL (control).

The dark toxicity of the complexes and conjugates was performed at concentration from 2.5 to 40 µg/mL, Fig. 10(A). In vitro dark toxicity is undesirable for photosensitizers aimed for use in PDT. The photosensitizers in Fig. 10(A) showed negligible dark toxicity on MCF-7 cells with cell viability percentage of above 90%. Upon analysis of triplicate replicate data of each concentration there was no statistically significant difference as the P-value was found to be greater than 0.05.

The MCF-7 cancer cells were treated with different concentrations (2.5–40 µg/mL) of NGQDs, B-NGQDs, 2, and 2-B-NGQDs upon irradiation with a Thorlabs M625L3 LED (625 nm) for 30 min to evaluate the photodynamic therapy activity of the complexes. Upon irradiation, the phototoxicity of NGQDs, B-NGQDs, 2, and 2-B-NGQDs against MCF-7 cells increased with the increase in concentration as shown in Fig. 10(B). The conjugates (2-B-NGQDs) exhibited higher PDT effect with cell viability of 22% at 40 μg/mL when compared to NGQDs alone with 25%, B-NGQDs alone with 30%, and 2 alone with 31% all at a concentration of 40 µg/mL. In PDT an excited triplet state of the photosensitizer transfers its energy to the ground state molecular oxygen resulting in the production of singlet oxygen, which is the main cytotoxic species in PDT, so as result of higher singlet oxygen quantum yield of 2-B-NGQDs corresponds to higher PDT effect. NGQDs also had high PDT effect because they are reported to be cytotoxic [10]. The IC50 values are given in Table 2 , and the IC50 values obtained for NGQDs, B-NGQDs, 2, and 2-B-NGQDs were 14.2, 14.4, 11.2, and 10.4 respectively. The IC50 values reported here are lower than for Sn(IV) porphyrins in the presence of peptide-based nanoparticles where the IC50 of 12.5 μg/mL was reported for the same cell line (MCF-7) [52]. The values in Table 2 are comparable to those studied for other porphyrins on MCF-7 cell line [53].

The cells were grown to 100% confluency and inoculated with samples at concentrations similar to those used for cytotoxicity and PDT studies and thereafter incubated over 24 h to allow for uptake into cells [8]. The cellular uptake of the porphyrin complexes and its conjugates was determined by measuring the absorbance of internalized porphyrin drug after incubating with MCF-7 cells for 24 h. The cellular uptake plays an important role in the PDT activity of the photosensitizer [54]. The cellular uptake increase was observed for the porphyrin complex, NGQDs and their conjugates upon an increase of their concentrations. Fig. 11 (A) shows that B-NGQDs has better cellular uptake (especially at low concentrations) compared to NGQDs, and this due to that cancer cells are known to overexpress biotin receptors [55], and Fig. 11(B) shows that 2-B-NGQDs performed better (especially at higher concentrations) than complex 2 alone which confirms that B-NGQDs enhanced complex 2 activity

The tin porphyrin complex (2) was synthesized and conjugated to biotin decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (B-NGQDs) through an axial ligand of the central metal (Sn (IV)) of 2. The
ϕ Δ
obtained were 0.59 and 0.79 for 2 and 2-B-NGQDs respectively in DMSO. Cellular uptake studies showed that 2-B-NGQDs exhibit better cellular uptake, and this correlated with PDT activity against MCF-7 cells. 2- B-NGQDs showed high phototoxicity with IC50 value of 10.4 µg/mL when compared to complex 2 alone, and we thus conclude that the biotin decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum enhanced the activity of complex 2.

N. Bridged Magaela: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Refilwe Matshitse: Methodology. Balaji Babu: Data curation. Muthumuni Managa: Methodology. Earl Prinsloo: Resources. Tebello Nyokong: Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) and Rhodes University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.poly.2021.115624.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
